frozen_components: base prover initialization (lightly finetuned on math data, used as KL reference)
architecture.summary: A game-theoretic training system that iteratively optimizes a verifier LM and role-conditioned prover LM to improve legibility of chain-of-thought solutions. The verifier (small LM) learns to predict solution correctness via supervised learning on balanced correct/incorrect samples. The prover (larger LM) is trained via RL with dual roles: helpful (optimized for correctness + verifier acceptance) and sneaky (optimized for incorrectness + verifier acceptance). Solutions flow as text; the verifier score feeds back as a reward signal to the prover. Across rounds, the data mixture feeding the verifier changes, but model weights carry over within each training phase.
trained_components: verifier language model (reward model for solution correctness)
helpful prover language model (conditioned role, RL-trained)
sneaky prover language model (conditioned role, RL-trained)

biblio.title: Prover-Verifier Games improve legibility of LLM outputs

claims.statement: Optimizing chain-of-thought solutions only for answer correctness can make them less legible.
When trained solely for correctness, human contractor accuracy at evaluating solution validity (45-second time limit) decreases from initialization to 7k RL steps.
The helpful prover's pass rate improves over checkability training rounds and human judges evaluate helpful solutions correctly more often at higher rounds.
Verifiers show increasing robustness across five rounds of checkability training, measured by steps required until sneaky-only adversaries find exploits.
Human contractor accuracy when evaluating sneaky prover solutions decreases over checkability training rounds, indicating flaws become more subtle.
Checkability training becomes unstable when using a verifier 0.01× the compute of the prover, producing weak provers with poor legibility.

Using a verifier that matches the size of the prover (1.0× compute) prevents the verifier from becoming more robust in subsequent rounds and produces provers with strong performance but poor legibility.

Legibility training against small verifiers transfers to legibility to humans: sneaky solutions harder to detect by small verifiers are also harder to detect by humans, and helpful solutions more legible to small verifiers are more legible to humans.
